heading: Assessment
html:
Falls in older adults are commonly multifactorial and assessment should identify modifiable contributors and risk of recurrence.

  • Falls are associated with significant morbidity and mortality.
  • Common contributors include orthostatic hypotension, polypharmacy, gait/balance impairment, cognitive impairment, and environmental hazards.
  • Orthostatic hypotension contributes to a meaningful proportion of syncope and is associated with increased mortality risk.

History

  • Clarify events before, during, and after the fall, with witness history where possible.
  • Record number of falls in the last 12 months.
  • Document loss of consciousness, injury, and ability to get up independently.
  • Check for orthostatic symptoms on standing: light-headedness, visual disturbance, posterior neck pain, syncope, fatigue, and trigger patterns (morning, heat, post-prandial).
  • Review medicines, cognition, continence, vision, footwear, and alcohol use.

Examination and bedside tests

  • Cardiovascular and neurological examination.
  • Lying and standing blood pressure using standard protocol.
  • ECG.
  • Gait and balance assessment (for example Timed Up and Go).
  • Clinical Frailty Score (Rockwood).

Blood tests (if indicated)

  • FBC, U&E, HbA1c, vitamin D, and TSH.

Lying and standing blood pressure protocol

  • Record blood pressure after at least 5 minutes lying.
  • Record within the first minute after standing.
  • Repeat at 3 minutes standing.
  • If blood pressure is still falling, repeat until stable.
  • Use a manual sphygmomanometer when possible (and if automated readings are unreliable).
  • Document associated symptoms during testing.

Positive orthostatic hypotension result (any one)

  • Systolic drop of at least 20 mmHg.
  • Standing systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg (with or without a 20 mmHg drop).
  • Diastolic drop of at least 10 mmHg with symptoms.

heading: Primary care management
html:
Medication review

  • Review and reduce or substitute medicines that increase falls or orthostatic risk where clinically appropriate.
  • Common contributors include antihypertensives (including alpha and beta blockers), diuretics, vasodilators, anti-Parkinson medicines, antidepressants, opioids, sedatives, anticholinergics, and other agents linked to autonomic dysfunction.
  • Reassess blood pressure and symptoms after medication changes.

Conservative orthostatic hypotension measures

  • Avoid prolonged standing, heat exposure, alcohol excess, large heavy meals, and prolonged bed rest.
  • Encourage fluid intake of approximately 2 to 4 litres daily if safe.
  • Use smaller frequent meals.
  • Encourage regular gentle exercise.
  • Raise the head of the bed by around 10 degrees.
  • Consider added dietary salt and compression garments if not contraindicated.
  • Teach symptom recognition and prompt sitting or lying when prodromal symptoms occur.

Self-management during symptoms

  • Sit down and take an oral water bolus of around 500 ml.
  • Use physical counter-pressure manoeuvres (for example handgrip, calf raises, leg crossing, squatting, whole-body tensing).
  • If symptoms persist, lie down with legs elevated.

Drug treatment

  • Fludrocortisone, midodrine, and pyridostigmine are normally initiated in secondary care when symptoms remain refractory.

Driving (DVLA)

  • Group 1: notification is generally not required for orthostatic symptoms occurring only on standing.
  • Group 2: any syncope requires driving cessation and DVLA notification.
  • Confirm current national DVLA guidance at point of care.

heading: Who to refer
html:
Hospital admission (same day)

  • Suspected cardiac syncope (for example exertional syncope, syncope without prodrome, abnormal ECG, or family history of sudden cardiac death).
  • Significant injury related to the fall (including head injury on anticoagulation or suspected fracture).
  • Acute focal neurological deficit or suspected TIA/stroke.
  • Suspected sepsis, gastrointestinal bleed, or significant volume depletion causing the fall.

Urgent suspicion of cancer (USOC)

  • No specific USOC criteria are defined in this pathway. Use local cancer referral pathways if red-flag cancer features are present.

Urgent (non-cancer outpatient)

  • Refer to Falls MDT (SCI Gateway to Geriatrics) when there are at least 2 falls in the last 12 months and at least one of the following: Clinical Frailty Score greater than 4, injury requiring medical/surgical treatment, loss of consciousness with the fall, or inability to get up independently.

Routine (outpatient)

  • Physiotherapy with or without Occupational Therapy via HomeTeams when MDT criteria are not met but gait or balance impairment is present.
  • DG Doing More referral when there has been a fall in the last year without gait or balance impairment.

Include in referral

  • Falls frequency over the preceding 12 months and key event details.
  • Injury history, loss of consciousness status, and ability to rise after a fall.
  • Lying and standing blood pressure results and associated symptoms.
  • Clinical Frailty Score (Rockwood), gait/balance findings, ECG result, and relevant blood results.
  • Medication review findings and interventions already attempted.

heading: Who not to refer
html:
• Single fall with a clearly identified reversible cause, no injury, and no gait or balance concern: manage in primary care with safety-netting.
  • Orthostatic hypotension that resolves with medication review and conservative measures, with no ongoing falls or gait impairment.
  • People in the last days of life where care goals are comfort focused; consider anticipatory care planning rather than Falls MDT referral.
  • Do not make routine Falls MDT referral when the threshold of at least 2 falls in 12 months plus additional qualifying criteria is not met.

heading: References
html:
• Local NHS Dumfries and Galloway Falls MDT pathway criteria.
  • Royal College of Physicians / FFFAP guidance on lying and standing blood pressure measurement.
  • NICE Clinical Knowledge Summaries (falls and orthostatic hypotension content where relevant).
  • RCGP orthostatic hypotension guidance.
  • Rockwood Clinical Frailty Scale.
  • DVLA medical standards for fitness to drive.
